bytes.topic: Process Management — process vs thread, scheduling algorithms, CFS
bytes.title: ebpf: Process Management in the Linux Kernel
bytes.body: When working with ebpf, understanding process management in the Linux kernel is crucial. A process is a separate instance of a program being executed in memory, while a thread is a flow of execution within the same memory space. In ebpf, we can use the `BPF_PROG_ATTACH` command to attach a program to a process. The Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) is used to schedule threads within a process. We can use the `bpf_cgroup_sched_cls_attach` function to attach a class to a cgroup, which allows us to schedule threads within a process.

bytes.title: ebpf: Leverage WebAssembly for System Access with WASI and Sandbox
bytes.body: ebpf programs interact with the Linux kernel's system call interface, but what if you need to access system resources outside of this interface? WebAssembly (WASM) provides a sandboxed execution environment for system calls, which can be leveraged using the WebAssembly System Interface (WASI). By using WASI, your ebpf programs can access system resources such as files, sockets, and processes, without compromising the secure execution environment of the kernel. This is achieved by mapping the system call entry points to the WASI interface, allowing ebpf programs to make system calls through the WASI API.

bytes.title: ebpf: Leveraging WASM on the Kernel with Wasmtime and Wasmer
bytes.body: You can use Wasmtime and Wasmer to execute WebAssembly (WASM) modules directly within the eBPF environment. This allows you to write kernel plugins in WASM for tasks like memory management, device drivers, or even kernel mode sandboxing. The `BPF_PROG_TYPE_WASM` type in eBPF allows for the creation of WASM programs, which can be loaded and executed using the `wasmtime` API. The `wasmer` crate provides a Rust implementation of the WASM interpreter, making it easier to integrate WASM code into your eBPF projects. By utilizing WASM on the kernel, you can leverage the flexibility and security of WebAssembly while still benefiting from the performance and security of the eBPF environment.

bytes.title: ebpf on Monolithic Kernels: A Hybrid Approach
bytes.body: When working with ebpf on monolithic kernels, developers often face challenges in modifying existing code. To overcome this, Linux provides a hybrid approach, where ebpf programs can be loaded as loadable kernel modules (LKMs). This allows developers to leverage LKM's ability to modify kernel behavior without modifying the core kernel code. In contrast, BSD's monolithic kernel design restricts ebpf's ability to load new code, forcing developers to rely on statically compiled kernel modules. For instance, an ebpf program can be compiled as an LKM and loaded into the Linux kernel using the 'insmod' command.

bytes.title: ebpf: Mastering Concurrency with Spinlocks and RCU
bytes.body: When working with ebpf programs, concurrency is a non-negotiable aspect. Two synchronization primitives, spinlocks and RCU, play a crucial role in maintaining thread safety. A spinlock is a lightweight lock that allows one thread to acquire the lock while others spin-wait until it's released. In contrast, RCU (Read-Copy Update) provides a way to safely update shared data without blocking readers. Memory barriers are also essential in ensuring visibility of changes across threads. ebpf provides the `BPF_spin_lock` and `BPF_rcu_lock` APIs for implementing these primitives. For atomic operations, ebpf's `BPF_atomic_add` and `BPF_atomic_sub` functions can be used.

bytes.title: ebpf: Enhanced Observability with BPF_PROG_ATTACH
bytes.body:
ebpf programs can be attached to various points in the Linux kernel using BPF_PROG_ATTACH. For observability, attach an ebpf program to the kretprobes, which allows you to monitor kernel function return values. This is particularly useful for tracking errors or anomalies. For example, you can attach an ebpf program to the kretprobe of the sys_open function to track the number of failed file opens.

You can use the kretprobe attach type with the BPF_PROG_ATTACH command in the bpftrace tool. The following code snippet demonstrates how to do this:

```bash
bpftrace -e 'kretprobe:sys_open / ret < 0 / { @[comm] = count(); }'
```
This code snippet attaches an ebpf program to the kretprobe of the sys_open function and increments a count for each failed file open.

bytes.title: ebpf: Optimizing Performance with Kernel Parameters and NUMA Tuning
bytes.body: When optimizing ebpf programs, kernel parameters and NUMA tuning are crucial for performance. Set the `numa_balancing` parameter to `0` to prevent the kernel from migrating tasks between NUMA nodes. Use `bpf_get_numa_node()` to determine the NUMA node where your ebpf program is running. To allocate huge pages, use `bpf_map_create()` with the `BPF_MAP_TYPE_LPM_TRIE` type and set the `map_flags` to `BPF_F_NUMA_NODE`. This allows your ebpf program to allocate huge pages on the same NUMA node, reducing memory access latency.
